title: Counting & Cardinality
objective: Students will explore counting & cardinality concepts including Rote counting to 100, Counting in 2s, Counting objects to 20 and more.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about Rote counting to 100. Get ready for an adventure!
Rote count forwards and backwards from 0 to 100, beginning from 0, 1, or any given number, by ones
Let's practice together! Recite the number sequence 1–100 without skipping or repeating
Can you count out loud all the way from 0 to 100 — and if you say "start from 47", can they carry on from there without going back to the beginning?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Rote counting to 100. Recite the number sequence 1–100 without skipping or repeating Count backward from 20 to 0
Let's explore Counting in 2s! Have you ever wondered about this?
Count in multiples of 2, 5, and 10 (skip counting)
Let me show you something cool. Count 2, 4, 6, 8 … up to at least 20
If you is counting socks being paired up, can they skip-count in twos — 2, 4, 6, 8 — all the way to 20? Can they also count up in fives when counting coins, or in tens on a number line?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Counting in 2s: Count 2, 4, 6, 8 … up to at least 20 Count 5, 10, 15, 20 … up to at least 50
Let's explore Counting objects to 20! Have you ever wondered about this?
Count a set of objects to answer 'how many?' for sets up to 20 (arranged in lines, arrays, circles, or scattered)
Let's practice together! Accurately count up to 20 objects in a line
If you spread out 15 buttons on the table in a random arrangement and ask you how many there are, can they count them all carefully and give you the right answer?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Counting objects to 20: Accurately count up to 20 objects in a line Count up to 10 scattered objects without losing track
Have you ever thought about Two written numerals between 1 and 10? Let's find out together!
Compare two written numerals between 1 and 10 to determine which is greater or less
Time to try this out: Given two written numerals (e.g. 4 and 7), identify which is greater
If you write "3" and "8" on two pieces of paper and ask you which number is bigger, can they get it right — and could they tell you why?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Two written numerals between 1 and 10: Given two written numerals (e.g. 4 and 7), identify which is greater Correctly use > and < or 'greater than' / 'less than' to compare single-digit numerals
